title: ExEF ESQ-R Interactive Intake
description: A 30-item, 5-area executive-function intake experience aligned to ExEF's current public ESQ-R framing. Includes one reverse-keyed validity item per area to detect straight-line responding.

areas.name: Planning and Time Management
areas.intro: This area looks at how clearly you map work, estimate time, and sequence steps before pressure builds.
areas.strengthSummary: You appear to create enough structure ahead of time to reduce last-minute chaos.
areas.growthSummary: The planning load may be landing too late, which can make reasonable work feel urgent or messy.
strategies: Define what done looks like before you begin.
Multiply first-draft estimates by 1.5 until you have real timing data.
Use visible checkpoints for any task that takes longer than one sitting.

areas.name: Task Initiation and Follow-Through
areas.intro: This area tracks how reliably you can begin work without extra pressure and keep momentum across multiple days.
areas.strengthSummary: Once a task matters, you are usually able to create traction and keep it moving.
areas.growthSummary: The main problem may be ignition rather than ability. Starts cost too much energy, so follow-through becomes fragile.
strategies: Choose one tiny visible first move before the work session starts.
Use a two-minute launch ritual so starting does not require a fresh decision each time.
End each day with a bridge note naming tomorrow's first action.

areas.name: Attention and Working Memory
areas.intro: This area covers staying with the task, holding key details in mind, and recovering after distraction.
areas.strengthSummary: You appear to hold task information well enough to stay oriented and recover after normal interruptions.
areas.growthSummary: Focus may be leaking through distraction, overload, or restart friction more than it first appears.
strategies: Stage only the materials for the current task before you begin.
Use a restart card after interruptions: where I was, next step, timer.
Externalize multi-step work with a checklist instead of relying on memory alone.

areas.name: Organization and Self-Monitoring
areas.intro: This area measures how well your systems stay usable and how quickly you notice when work is drifting off course.
areas.strengthSummary: Your systems appear visible enough to support consistency without constant cleanup.
areas.growthSummary: The friction may be less about effort and more about weak external systems or late error detection.
strategies: Create one home for tasks, one home for materials, and one weekly reset.
Add checkpoints that ask what is working, what is off, and what changes now.
Review your calendar and task list together instead of in separate silos.

areas.name: Emotional Regulation and Flexibility
areas.intro: This area tracks how strongly emotion, uncertainty, or sudden changes disrupt performance and recovery.
areas.strengthSummary: You appear able to stay fairly steady under shifting conditions and return to task after stress.
areas.growthSummary: Stress, disappointment, or changing expectations may be draining more execution energy than others can see.
strategies: Name the emotional state first, then pick the next concrete move.
Pre-plan a fallback option for high-friction tasks.
Use short reset routines after stress instead of asking yourself to push straight through.

questions.prompt: Before I begin a multi-step task, I usually know what the finished version should look like.
My schedule leaves enough room for transitions, setup time, and normal interruptions.
I can break large projects into smaller steps without getting stuck in the middle.
I usually estimate task length realistically instead of planning for ideal conditions only.
When deadlines move closer, my plan still mostly holds instead of collapsing.
I can start important work without needing panic, shame, or someone watching me.
If I know the first step, I can usually begin within a reasonable window.
I stay with longer projects instead of losing momentum after the first burst.
If I miss a day, I can restart the next day without a major reset.
I do not rely on deadline pressure as my main way to create action.
I can stay with low-interest work without my attention sliding away quickly.
I hold the key details of a task in mind while I work.
After an interruption, I can get back on track without a long restart delay.
Noise, clutter, or notifications do not pull me off course as easily as they used to.
I finish routine tasks without forgetting steps or losing the thread halfway through.
My task list, calendar, and materials are organized enough that I can trust them.
I notice fairly quickly when a plan is drifting and can correct it before things snowball.
Important materials, files, or links are usually where I expect them to be.
I review priorities often enough that avoidable surprises stay limited.
My systems still work when life gets busy, rather than only on calm days.
Strong feelings do not usually knock me out of action for long.
When plans change, I can adapt without losing the whole day.
I can handle feedback without shutting down, spiraling, or over-correcting.
Pressure makes me more focused more often than it makes me freeze.
If something goes wrong, I can recover and move to the next step without staying stuck there.
When my plan meets reality, it usually falls apart instead of holding up.
I tend to wait until deadline pressure forces me to start important work.
I often lose track of what I was doing partway through a task.
My systems break down whenever life gets unexpectedly busy.
When something goes wrong, I get stuck replaying it instead of moving on.
